dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Convulsions/Seizure at a residence in the city of Richmond.
Hi, I'm Chris, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened that made you call 911?
She had a seizure in the activity room. It lasted about 30 seconds to a minute, and she's been on the floor since.
Ma'am, can you hear me right now? What is your name?
My name is Jennifer.
Can you point to what city we are in?
Okay, uh, we're in Richmond.
You do not currently have any airway blockage. Airway is clear.
Respirations are 20, evenly rising, and unlabored. No signs of distress.
Pulse is 70. Skin is warm, pink, and dry. Blood pressure is 142/59.
When did the seizure take place?
Around 10:30. She has Down syndrome and a history of seizures. This was the second one within the last year.
You're not in pain right now. What did she eat or drink recently?
Bystanders couldn't confirm time of last intake or if she vomited, but she did have a doll with her during the seizure.
After the seizure, did you notice any discomfort?
She's alert but quiet. On a scale of 0 to 10, it's a mild 4/10 dull pain.
She's a 55-year-old woman with a baseline GCS of 14. She appears oriented and likely in a postictal state.
Nose is clear. Mouth is clear. No drainage or obstruction.
Chest movement is equal and unlabored in both sides. Lung sounds are clear in all lobes.
Pupils are equal and reactive to light.
No injuries noted to arms or legs. Any additional pain?
Skin is still warm, pink, and dry. No rashes or cuts, but she does have a history of hyperlipidemia.
Pulse is currently 70. Strong and regular.
Respirations are 20. Depth is within normal range.
Blood pressure is 142/59.
SpO2 reading started at 98%.
Glucose was unrecordable but possibly due to a faulty reading.
Peripheral pulses are strong and regular in all extremities.
We've applied a 4-lead ECG and started an IV in the left arm for post-seizure monitoring.
We are following Protocol 3-12 for medical-seizure. Transporting to St. Mary's.
Pulse has increased to 80. EKG is in sinus rhythm. Pain remains 4/10.
Respirations still at 20, ECG normal. BP is now 139/81.
Glucose is now at 120. It was likely a faulty reading earlier.
BP fell to 120/76 and RR increased to 21. EKG is still in sinus rhythm.
Is your pain still 4/10, or has it changed?
No new symptoms. She remains quiet after the seizure.
We'll place you on the stretcher and secure it with a harness for safety.
We're taking you to St. Mary's. Comfortable with that?
She is now on a 5-point harness with IV in the left arm. Vital signs are stable, SpO2 at 96%, and EKG in sinus rhythm.
Bystanders couldn't confirm the last meal or fluid intake, but there were no signs of difficulty swallowing.
She had lunch a few hours earlier. No vomiting or difficulty swallowing was reported.
Head and neck show no signs of trauma. No swelling, deformity, or discoloration.
Abdominal exam found soft and non-tender. No distension or masses observed.
No trauma or bleeding in the genital area observed.
Skin remains intact, warm, and dry. No lesions or rashes noted.
SpO2 improved to 96% and remains stable.
EKG still in sinus rhythm with no signs of dysrhythmia.
Glucose is at 120 and trending. We'll monitor it closely.
Pulse is 80, respirations 20, and BP is 139/81. All stable.
Any increase in pain or added discomfort since being moved to the stretcher?
No. She was stable on arrival and remains so now.
Can you squeeze on my fingers? Thank you. No numbness or loss of sensation reported.
Pupils remain equal and reactive. No foreign bodies detected.
She's taking citalopram, levothyroxine, and alendronate. No antiepileptics on hand today.
IV remains patent and ECG active throughout.
Double-checked EKG again. Still normal rhythm.
IV access is functional and fully patent with no issues.
BP is now 120/76. Respirations 21. SpO2 at 98%. Continued stability.
Pain level remains at 4/10, noted in the head and knee.
All restraints are secure. Patient is stable.
Transport is continuing. No sign of changes in status or instability.
Any known risk for seizure recurrence at this time?
She takes citalopram, levothyroxine, and alendronate. No specific antiepileptics maintained.
Any issues with hearing, drainage, or throat discomfort since the seizure?
No trauma noted. Head pain reported but no swelling or other injury observed.
No pain found upon palpation. Chest moves uniformly with breathing.
She moved independently after the seizure. No sudden change in extremity function reported.
Latest pulse measurement remains stable at 72.
EKG reading updated at 11:55 and confirmed in sinus rhythm.
Head pain is still reported at 4/10. Some fluctuation observed.
No reported issues beyond initial local pain. No new injuries identified.
Her back has full range of motion and normal appearance.
Skin remains intact without indication of temperature changes or pressure points.
IV monitoring is still active. No other interventions required at this time.
SpO2 is again at 98% in the postictal phase.
Glucose remains stable at 120. No need for dextrose at this point.
IV access remains secure and functional.
Any concerns with repositioning while en route?
Anticipated arrival is 20 minutes. Monitor for any seizure activity or changes in vital signs.
Here are the details: 55-year-old female, named Jennifer. No known allergies listed in provided report.
She was last in contact with citalopram. No immediate adverse interactions reported.
Onset was around 10:30 and has continued since.
Verbal reports are consistent and aligned with current GCS.
BP is trending low normal around 120/76. Being monitored within safe margins.
All restraints in place. Stretcher secured and patient ready.
No new medical conditions noted. Transport remains the main course.
Patient can be transferred to ambulance once in position, if safely possible.
Any movement, speech, or positioning provokes the 4/10 pain?
Pain is localized and did not spread to other areas.
Patient remains comfortable with safe restraints in place.
IV remains in place with full patency and no reported issues.
Back shows full skin integrity with no visible signs of contusion.
Pulse is 72, consistent with baseline and stable.
Stretcher position confirmed with 5-point harness intact.
Pain remained consistent at 4/10. Stable since EMS arrival.
No reported psychiatric needs or behavioral issues. Patient is non-combative.
Patient will be transferred to the ED via stretcher-to-bed sliding method.
All restraints are fully in compliance with transport protocol.
Ready for handoff. Full medical record and current IV details will be delivered.
Departure in five minutes to ensure timely care remains active.
Equipment verified. No issues found.
Departure to St. Mary's has begun per documentation.
IV and monitoring will continue while in ambulance under 5-point harness.
Patient information and care report now in the hands of ED for review.
All legal documentation is reviewed and complete for transport.
Equipment fully secured. Transport begins.
Travel time is approximately 30 minutes. Maintain awareness of vital signs per readings.
Patient now positioned in ambulance. All restraints and monitoring equipment active.
ED nurse has been alerted. Patient in transition.
EMS duties completed. Patient now in care with verified medical records at site.
